title: Blockhead Hans
summary: A story about a man who is considered a fool by society, but whose simple, honest nature and unwavering kindness lead to an unexpected positive outcome and the realization of his true worth.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of Blockhead Hans is told. A story about a man who is considered a fool by society, but whose simple, honest nature and unwavering kindness lead to an unexpected positive outcome and the realization of his true worth. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: Kindness is always rewarded in the end.

title: Bárðar Saga - The Guardian of Snæfell
summary: Bárðr, born of a human mother and a father who was half-giant and half-troll, settles in Snæfellsnes. After a series of family conflicts involving his brother and children, Bárðr gives away his land and vanishes into the Snæfellsjökull ice cap. He becomes the guardian spirit of the region, appearing in a grey cowl to help those in need during times of great difficulty.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of Bárðar Saga - The Guardian of Snæfell has been told for generations. Bárðr, born of a human mother and a father who was half-giant and half-troll, settles in Snæfellsnes. After a series of family conflicts involving his brother and children, Bárðr gives away his land and vanishes into the Snæfellsjökull ice cap. He becomes the guardian spirit of the region, appearing in a grey cowl to help those in need during times of great difficulty. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: Bárðar Saga - The Mound of King Raknarr
summary: Gestr, the son of Bárðr, journeys to the far north to challenge the undead King Raknarr. With the help of a priest and a magic candle, Gestr enters Raknarr's burial mound and beheads the king and his 500 undead crew. However, the magic candle fails, and Gestr is only saved by the divine light of King Olaf, who drains the undead of their strength.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of Bárðar Saga - The Mound of King Raknarr has been told for generations. Gestr, the son of Bárðr, journeys to the far north to challenge the undead King Raknarr. With the help of a priest and a magic candle, Gestr enters Raknarr's burial mound and beheads the king and his 500 undead crew. However, the magic candle fails, and Gestr is only saved by the divine light of King Olaf, who drains the undead of their strength. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: True strength comes from the heart, not just the muscles.

title: Beaivi: The Sun Goddess's Journey
summary: The myth of Beaivi, the Sámi sun goddess who travels with her daughter through the sky in an enclosure covered with reindeer bones and antlers, bringing spring. In winter, she disappears below the horizon for two months in the far north. On the winter solstice, white female reindeer were sacrificed to ensure her return. Butter was smeared on doorposts to strengthen her. Madness was believed to result from her absence during the dark winter months.
full_text: Beaivi, the sun goddess, rides through the sky in an enclosure made of reindeer bones and antlers. Beside her sits Beaivi-nieida, her daughter, who helps her guide the sun across the heavens. In summer, Beaivi's light never fades in the far north—she circles the sky without setting, warming the earth and stirring the plants to life. But in winter, she grows tired. For two months she disappears below the horizon, leaving the Sámi in darkness. During this time, madness can take hold. The dark presses against the mind, and those who cannot bear it may lose themselves. On the winter solstice, the Sámi performed the Festival of Beaivi. White female reindeer were sacrificed to give Beaivi strength. Their meat was threaded on sticks bent into rings and tied with bright ribbons. Butter was smeared on doorposts so that Beaivi would find nourishment as she passed. The ritual ensured her return, and when the sun's light crept back over the horizon, the Sámi rejoiced, for Beaivi had come home.
moral: Light always returns after darkness, but it must be welcomed with reverence and ritual.

title: Bieggolmmái: The Wind God's Shovels
summary: Bieggolmmái (the Wind Man) is the god of wind who lives in a cave. He holds two shovels—one to release wind from his cave and one to put it back. According to the Nærøy manuscript, he had a club in his left hand to chase the wind back. He was crucial for reindeer herding because reindeer travel into headwinds to avoid mosquitoes. Sacrifices of horns were made at Piegg-oaivadz fells. Tiny birch-bark boats with offerings were set adrift as sacrifices.
full_text: Deep inside a cave on the fells, Bieggolmmái the Wind Man sits on his throne of stone. In his right hand he holds a shovel that releases wind from the cave. In his left hand he holds a shovel that puts the wind back. When he grows angry, he swings both shovels with fury, and storms rage across the tundra. The Nærøy manuscript describes him with a club in his left hand, chasing the unruly wind back into its prison. For the Sámi reindeer herders, Bieggolmmái was both friend and danger. Reindeer travel into the wind to escape mosquitoes, so a steady wind meant good grazing. But a sudden storm could scatter a herd across the fells. At Piegg-oaivadz in Inari, Finland, Sámi made offerings of reindeer horns to Bieggolmmái. They built tiny birch-bark boats, filled them with offerings, and set them adrift on the wind. A famous photographer, lost in mist on Kiruna mountain, prayed to Bieggolmmái. The wind cleared his path and guided him home. To this day, the Sámi remember to thank the Wind Man for his favor.
moral: Even the wildest forces of nature can be appeased with respect and offerings.
source: Johan Randulf Nærøy manuscript; beneathnorthernlights.com; Wikipedia

title: Big Water Creature and the Great Flood
summary: In the Third World, after the reunion of men and women, a mother and her two daughters missed the raft crossing and swam the river. Big Water Creature (Tééhoołtsódii) seized the daughters and dragged them underwater. Talking God and Water Sprinkler created bowls of white and blue shell that spun an opening to an underwater house. First Man and First Woman rescued the girls while Coyote (First Angry) stole Big Water Creature's own two children. In retaliation, Big Water Creature sent a great flood from all directions. The people fled to the sacred mountain Sisnaajiní, where First Man planted the Male Reed. Woodpecker hollowed the reed, and the people climbed into the Fourth World through its passage, just ahead of the rising waters.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: Baaxpée and the Vision Quest
summary: Baaxpée is the sacred power of God (Akbaatatdia) in Crow belief, meaning 'power transcending the ordinary.' To acquire Baaxpée, a Crow must go on a vision quest (bilisshíissanne—'to fast from water'). The quester goes alone to an isolated peak, usually in the Wolf Mountains, fasting without food or water for two to three days. Self-mortification, including finger removal, is sometimes offered to show devotion. When a spirit pities the quester, it induces a vision, adopting the Crow as a child and bestowing Baaxpée. The spirit becomes a 'Medicine Father' who guides the Crow throughout life. The quester creates a Xapáaliia (medicine object) to represent the bond.

title: Bukwus the Wild Man of the Woods (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Bukwus is the male counterpart of D'Sonoqua, the Wild Man of the Woods in Kwakwaka'wakw mythology. He is a hairy, giant being who lives in the deep forests and is associated with the spirits of those who died by starvation or exposure. Bukwus roams the woods calling out in a hollow voice, trying to lure humans into the forest with promises of food. He is particularly dangerous because he appears as a starving, pitiful figure who begs for help, but anyone who follows him becomes lost forever and joins his ghostly company. Unlike D'Sonoqua, who is more easily outwitted, Bukwus is treacherous and deceptive. However, like D'Sonoqua, he is also associated with great wealth and can bestow supernatural powers upon those who manage to survive an encounter with him. Bukwus appears in both the T'seka (Red Cedar Bark Ceremonies) and the Tła'sala (Peace Dances), where dancers wear masks depicting his gaunt, haunted features.
source: Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography; Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition

title: Blue Jay Visits Ghost Town (Chinook - Ioi and Blue Jay)
summary: Blue Jay's sister Ioi was bought by ghosts as a wife and disappeared to the land of the dead. After a year, Blue Jay went searching for her. He asked all the trees and birds where people go when they die. He journeyed to the ghost town, which appeared to him as a place of bones and decay, but the ghosts saw it as beautiful. Ioi warned Blue Jay not to laugh at the ghosts or behave disrespectfully, but Blue Jay could not control himself. He kicked skulls, laughed at the skeletons, and shouted at the ghost people who were only skeletons to his eyes. When the ghosts went to butcher a whale, Blue Jay saw only a beached skeleton. When they brought in firewood, he saw only bark. Ioi kept explaining that his eyes saw differently because he was alive — the ghosts saw everything as beautiful. He tried to join the ghost dances, but when the ghosts danced gracefully, Blue Jay stood on his head. He died a second time and remained in the ghost world forever. This haunting story explores the boundary between life and death and the dangers of lacking respect for the supernatural world.
source: Chinook Texts, Smithsonian Institution; Chinook oral tradition from Charles Cultee

title: Blue Jay Finds a Wife (Chinook)
summary: Ioi decided it was high time for Blue Jay to quit his playful trickery and settle down with a wife. She told him he must select a wife from the land of the dead, from the Supernatural People. Ioi recommended that Blue Jay choose an old woman and suggested the recently deceased wife of a chief. Blue Jay went to the ghost town and courted the ghost woman. True to his nature, he behaved foolishly, misinterpreting the customs of the dead and acting disrespectfully. He made inappropriate jokes and did not understand that the ghosts perceived reality differently than the living. Despite Ioi's warnings, Blue Jay's behavior caused chaos in the ghost village. The story highlights Blue Jay's inability to mature and learn from experience, making him a comic but ultimately tragic figure. It is also one of several Chinook stories that explore the relationship between the living and the dead, suggesting that the boundary between worlds must be approached with humility and respect.

title: Brer Rabbit and the Briar Patch (Bred and Bawn)
summary: After Brer Fox captures Brer Rabbit using the Tar-Baby, he declares his intent to kill and eat him. Brer Rabbit pleads for his life, begging not to be thrown into the briar patch, suggesting instead that he be roasted, hanged, drowned, or skinned. The more Brer Rabbit begs to avoid the briar patch, the more determined Brer Fox becomes to throw him there. Finally, Brer Fox snatches Brer Rabbit by the hind legs and flings him deep into the briar patch. To Brer Fox's astonishment, Brer Rabbit appears on a hillside, picking burrs from his fur, and shouts back: 'Bred en bawn in a brier-patch, Brer Fox—bred en bawn in a brier-patch!' The briar patch, which would tear the skin of any other creature, was Brer Rabbit's ancestral home.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Rabbit's Laughing Place
summary: Brer Fox catches Brer Rabbit and declares he will finally kill him. Brer Rabbit, always quick-witted, says he does not care about dying but begs to be allowed to visit his 'laughing place' one last time to die happy. Curious, Brer Fox agrees and follows Brer Rabbit into the woods. Brer Rabbit leads him to a hollow tree filled with a swarm of angry bees. Brer Rabbit points to the tree and whispers that his laughing place is inside. When Brer Fox peers into the hollow, the bees swarm out and attack him furiously. While Brer Fox flees in agony, Brer Rabbit laughs himself silly and escapes once again, proving that his most dangerous weapon is his clever tongue.
source: African American oral tradition, collected in Uncle Remus stories

title: Brer Rabbit and the Moon in the Millpond
summary: A classic African American trickster tale in which Brer Rabbit, feeling thirsty, goes to the millpond to drink and sees the moon reflected in the water. Mistaking it for a golden cheese wheel, he tries to retrieve it. Unable to reach it from the bank, he tricks Brer Fox into helping him by claiming there is a valuable treasure in the pond. Brer Fox ties a rope around Brer Rabbit's waist and lowers him down, but Brer Rabbit only stirs up the water, making the reflection disappear. He claims the treasure sank and convinces Brer Fox to jump in after it, leaving Brer Fox cold and humiliated while Brer Rabbit laughs and feasts on the cheese he finds elsewhere.
source: African American oral tradition (variant collected throughout the US South)

title: Brer Rabbit Nibbles Up the Butter
summary: Brer Rabbit, Brer Fox, Brer Possum, and Brer Coon together churn butter and set it aside to share later. Brer Rabbit grows hungry and secretly eats all the butter. When the friends discover the butter is gone, Brer Rabbit blames Brer Possum and suggests they test who is guilty by having each lie down in the sun and see whose belly the butter melts from. Brer Rabbit arranges things so that Brer Possum ends up sitting on the butter smear, and when the sun warms him, the butter appears on his fur. Brer Possum is punished for Brer Rabbit's crime, while Brer Rabbit escapes blame through clever manipulation. The story explains why the possum plays dead when caught—he learned to be wary of being tricked again.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Rabbit Rides Brer Fox
summary: Brer Rabbit, seeking to prove his superiority, convinces Miss Meadows and the girls that he can ride Brer Fox like a horse. Brer Fox overhears this boast and vows revenge. The next day, Brer Rabbit pretends to be sick and asks Brer Fox to carry him to Miss Meadows's house. Once on Brer Fox's back, Brer Rabbit digs in his heels and rides Brer Fox straight to the gathering, where he shows off by declaring: 'This is the way I ride Brer Fox!' Humiliated, Brer Fox shakes him off, but Brer Rabbit escapes into a hollow tree. Brer Fox enlists Brer Wolf to help dig him out, but Brer Rabbit tricks them by claiming enemies are approaching and sends them fleeing. The tale is part of a cycle of African trickster stories also found in the Caribbean and South America.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Terrapin Outruns Brer Rabbit
summary: Brer Rabbit, proud of his speed, mocks Brer Terrapin for his slow, plodding pace. Brer Terrapin challenges Brer Rabbit to a race. Knowing he cannot win on speed alone, Brer Terrapin stations his wife and several family members at intervals along the race course. At each mile marker, Brer Rabbit calls out 'Brer Terrapin!' and one of the terrapin family calls back, 'Here I am!' After several such exchanges, Brer Rabbit grows exhausted and confused, unable to understand how the slow terrapin keeps ahead of him. When Brer Terrapin crosses the finish line first, Brer Rabbit collapses from exhaustion. The story—a variant of the tortoise and hare theme—is a cornerstone of African American animal folklore, reinforcing that patience and strategy triumph over arrogance.
source: African American oral tradition (Gullah Geechee and Uncle Remus variants)

title: Brer Rabbit Finds His Match at Last
summary: Brer Rabbit, overconfident after countless successful tricks, encounters a creature he cannot outsmart. In some versions, it is Old Man Tarrypin (the terrapin) who turns the tables on Brer Rabbit; snip in others, it is a wise old woman or a young girl who matches wits with him. Brer Rabbit tries every trick in his repertoire—false friendliness, reverse psychology, flattery, and fear—but nothing works. The creature remains calm, sees through every deception, and ultimately traps Brer Rabbit in a situation where his cleverness fails him. Brer Rabbit must humble himself, admit defeat, and negotiate his release. This tale serves as a caution that even the most cunning trickster may eventually meet his match, reminding listeners that overconfidence is the trickster's own greatest vulnerability.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Cooter (Terrapin) and the Deer: The Great Race
summary: In this Gullah Geechee variant of the classic trickster race tale, Brer Cooter (the terrapin) and Brer Deer are both courting the same young lady. She declares she will marry whichever one wins a ten-mile race. Brer Deer laughs at the slow-moving terrapin, but Brer Cooter is clever. He stations nine members of his family at each mile marker along the race course, and he himself waits at the start right in front of the young lady's door. At nine o'clock, the race begins. Brer Deer springs forward while Brer Cooter disappears. At the first mile marker, Brer Deer calls out, 'Brer Cooter!' A voice answers from the broomgrass, 'I'm here too!' At every mile post, the same thing happens: Brer Deer calls out, and a terrapin voice answers. By the ninth mile, Brer Deer believes he is about to tie with Brer Cooter. But at the finish line, Brer Cooter is already there, having won the race and the young lady's hand. The tale reinforces that speed alone does not win—strategy, family, and preparation are just as important as raw ability.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip collected by Joel Chandler Harris in Uncle Remus introduction (1881)

title: Bouki the Hyena's Greed (Wolof)
summary: Bouki the Hyena found a field of ripe watermelons and decided to eat them all. But each time he took a bite, the vine made a creaking sound that terrified him because he thought it was a farmer approaching. Bouki became so paranoid that he refused to eat alone and begged Leuk the Hare to join him. Leuk agreed on one condition: Bouki must close his eyes and not open them until Leuk said so. Leuk then ate all the best watermelons while Bouki sat with his eyes closed. When Bouki finally opened his eyes, only the rotten rinds remained. Bouki complained, but Leuk shrugged and said that greedy creatures do not deserve good things. The story serves as a cautionary tale about greed and the importance of sharing. Bouki's constant hunger and foolishness represent the consequences of selfishness in Wolof moral teaching.

title: Bayajidda Slays the Serpent of Daura
summary: The foundational legend of the Hausa people tells of Bayajidda (Abu Yazid), a prince from Baghdad who traveled across Africa to Daura, where a fearsome serpent named Sarki guarded the town's only well, the Kusugu well. The serpent only allowed the people to draw water on Fridays. Bayajidda, unaware of the danger, went to the well on a Thursday. When the serpent attacked, he cut off its head with his sword. As a reward, Queen Daurama offered him half her kingdom, but Bayajidda instead asked for her hand in marriage, ending the tradition of celibate queens. Their son Bawo fathered six sons who, along with Bayajidda's first son Biram, became the founders of the seven Hausa states: Daura, Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Rano, Gobir, and Biram.

title: Baiame the Sky Father
summary: Baiame is the supreme creator god and sky father in the mythology of several Aboriginal Australian peoples of southeastern Australia, including the Kamilaroi, Wiradjuri, and Eora nations. In the Dreamtime, Baiame descended from the sky world to create the land, plants, animals, and humans. He gave the people their laws, customs, and ceremonies before returning to the sky, where he remains watching over the world from his home in the Milky Way. Baiame established the rules of marriage, the totem system, and the proper relationships between people and land. His wives include Birrahgnooloo (associated with fertility and floods) and Cunnembeillee. Baiame is portrayed in rock art and carvings, often depicted with enormous size and radiating headgear suggesting divine power. His son Daramulum acts as an intermediary between Baiame and humans.

title: Bukha Noyon the Bull God
summary: A Buryat myth about Bukha Noyon, the sacred bull god who descended from the sky to create the world. In the beginning, the sky god sent Bukha Noyon, a massive celestial bull, down to the primordial waters. The bull dredged up mud from the bottom with his horns, which the creator used to form the earth. Bukha Noyon then became the progenitor of the Buryat people, particularly the western Buryat clans. He is associated with fertility, strength, and the mountains of the Baikal region. Sacred sites dedicated to Bukha Noyon still exist in Buryatia, where traditional offerings are made. The bull is considered the totemic ancestor and protector of the Buryat people, and his legend reinforces the strong pastoral traditions of Buryat nomadic culture.

title: Bakunawa: The Moon-Eating Serpent
summary: In Visayan mythology, Bakunawa is a massive sea serpent or dragon believed to cause eclipses by attempting to swallow the moon. Originally a beautiful sea goddess, Bakunawa was transformed into a serpent as punishment for falling in love with a mortal. Each night she rises from the ocean depths to chase the moon across the sky. When she catches it, the people must make loud noises with pots and pans to frighten her away, causing her to release the moon. This ancient myth explains lunar eclipses and reflects the deep connection between Filipino ancestors and celestial observation.
notes: Bakunawa is notably absent from creature DB. Should be added as a Philippine dragon/serpent type.
moral: The natural and spiritual world reveal divine truths to those who seek them.

title: Bawang Merah Bawang Putih (Shallot and Garlic)
summary: A popular Javanese folktale of two stepsisters: Bawang Putih (Garlic), who is kind, diligent, and beautiful, and Bawang Merah (Shallot), who is spoiled, lazy, and cruel. After their father dies, Bawang Putih is mistreated by her stepmother and stepsister. While washing clothes in the river, she accidentally loses a garment and meets an old woman who returns it after Bawang Putih helps her with chores. The old woman rewards her with a pumpkin containing jewels. Greedy Bawang Merah replicates the journey but behaves rudely, receiving a pumpkin filled with snakes and scorpions. The story teaches that kindness is always rewarded while greed and cruelty bring misfortune.
notes: The Indonesian equivalent of 'Cinderella' — one of the most widely told tales in the archipelago.
moral: Kindness is always rewarded while cruelty brings its own destruction.

title: Badang the Strongman
summary: Badang is the legendary Malay strongman from ancient Singapura (pre-colonial Singapore) whose feats of strength are celebrated in the Malay Annals (Sejarah Melayu). Originally a slave, Badang gained superhuman strength after consuming the regurgitated vomit of a river spirit — a grotesque but powerful magical substance he had declined to eat before being tricked into it. His strength became legendary: he could lift boats single-handedly, uproot trees, and hurl massive boulders. The Raja employed him as the royal strongman, and he famously defeated a champion from India in a strength contest by lifting an impossibly heavy stone. This stone, known as the Singapore Stone, once stood at the mouth of the Singapore River.
notes: Connected to the historical Singapore Stone, which bore ancient inscriptions.
moral: True honor lives on in stories passed down through generations.

title: Batu Belah Batu Bertangkup
summary: A classic Malay folktale from Perak about a mother who abandons her children after being seduced by a forest spirit. The story follows two siblings, a brother and a sister, left to fend for themselves when their mother disappears into the jungle with a mysterious man. The children wander alone until they encounter a massive split rock called Batu Belah Batu Bertangkup — a sentient stone that opens and closes like a mouth, devouring anyone who passes too close. The sister is swallowed by the rock, but the resourceful brother uses trickery and the help of forest animals to rescue her. The story teaches children about responsibility, family loyalty, and the dangers of trusting strangers.
notes: A well-known Perak folktale with multiple variants. Warnings against parental neglect.
moral: Honor your parents, for ingratitude brings divine punishment.

title: Bukit Shahbandar (The Warning Hill)
summary: A Bruneian legend about Datu Mansur, a corrupt Shahbandar (harbor master) who abused his authority by overtaxing merchants and stealing from the Sultan's treasury. The people suffered under his greed, but none dared speak against him. An elderly village bomoh (shaman) named Nenek Fatimah warned him that the ancestors would not tolerate his corruption. When Datu Mansur dismissed her and increased his oppression, supernatural storms gathered over his hilltop settlement. Lightning struck the ground, the earth trembled, and the entire settlement — including Datu Mansur — was transformed into stone. The hill became a permanent warning to those who abuse power. Today, Bukit Shahbandar stands as a reminder of divine justice and the consequences of corruption in leadership.
notes: Explains the origin of a real geographic feature in Brunei. Moral: corruption leads to destruction.
moral: True honor lives on in stories passed down through generations.

title: Behula and Lakhindar — The Serpent Goddess's Trial
summary: Chand Saudagar, a wealthy merchant and devotee of Shiva, refused to worship the serpent goddess Manasa. In revenge, Manasa killed his six elder sons by snakebite. His last son, Lakhindar, was married to Behula in a specially constructed iron chamber meant to protect him from snakes. But on the wedding night, Manasa sent a snake that entered through a tiny crevice and killed Lakhindar. Refusing to accept her husband's death, Behula placed his body on a banana-leaf raft and floated down the river for nine months, enduring storms and dangers, until she reached the court of the gods. Her unwavering devotion moved Manasa, who restored Lakhindar and all his brothers to life. Chand finally acknowledged Manasa's divinity. Behula is celebrated in Bengali culture as the epitome of wifely devotion.
source: Manasamangal Kavya, Bengali medieval epic (13th-18th centuries)
moral: Unwavering devotion moves heaven and earth

title: Bonbibi – The Lady of the Sundarbans Forest
summary: Bonbibi is the guardian spirit of the Sundarbans mangrove forest, worshipped by both Hindus and Muslims in Bengal and Bangladesh. According to legend, she was sent by Allah to protect forest-dwellers from the demon-king Dakkhin Rai, who ruled the forest in the form of a tiger and demanded human sacrifices. Bonbibi, accompanied by her brother Shah Jangali, fought and defeated Dakkhin Rai, forcing him to agree never to harm those who called upon Bonbibi's name. Before entering the forest to collect honey or wood, locals pray to Bonbibi for protection against tigers, and her shrines are found throughout the Sundarbans. She represents the syncretic folk traditions of Bengal where Hindu and Muslim practices blend seamlessly.
source: Bonbibir Gaan (oral ballad tradition of the Sundarbans)
moral: True honor lives on in stories

title: Bhil Origin Story — The Birth of the Bhil People
summary: The Bhil tribe, one of India's largest Adivasi groups, traces its origin to the god Mahadev (Shiva). According to the myth, Mahadev was roaming the forest when he created a bow and arrow to teach humanity survival. The first Bhil was born from the sweat of Mahadev's brow. Another version tells that the goddess Parvati created the first Bhil woman from her combings, and Mahadev created the first Bhil man from his nail parings. The bow and arrow (dhanush and teer) is central to Bhil identity — every Bhil is traditionally a skilled archer. The legend of Bhilna Dev and the tiger, in which a Bhil hero tames a man-eating tiger using only his bow and his wits, remains a central cultural story. The Bhil goddess Bhadbhavi is the protector of forests and is invoked before hunting expeditions.
source: Bhil oral traditions, central and western Indian tribal folklore
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Bodu Naga: The Serpent Spirit of the Lagoon
summary: Bodu Naga is a great serpent spirit in Maldivian folklore inhabiting lagoons, reefs, and deep channels between islands. In pre-Islamic Maldives, Nagas were revered as powerful nature spirits associated with the waters that surrounded the atolls. Fishermen offered prayers and small offerings before venturing into waters believed to be guarded by Bodu Naga to ensure safe passage and abundant catch. The entity is part of a broader Maldivian tradition of spirit beings (handi, fureytha, dhevi) that could appear in human form — often as beautiful women or well-built men — to lure humans. The Bodu Naga legend reflects the ancient Dravidian naga worship that existed in the Maldives before Buddhism and Islam, and shares similarities with serpent traditions across South India and Sri Lanka. The nagas were said to have been subdued as Buddhism spread through the Indian Ocean, but their stories persist in folklore about dangerous waters and forbidden reefs.
source: Xavier Romero-Frias 'Folk Tales of the Maldives', Maldivian oral maritime tradition
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Baron Samedi, Lord of the Dead
summary: Baron Samedi (Baron Saturday) is one of the most iconic lwa of Haitian Vodou, the master of death, tombs, graveyards, and resurrection. He is depicted wearing a top hat, black tail coat, dark glasses, and cotton plugs in the nostrils like a corpse prepared for burial. Often shown as a skeleton, he speaks in a nasal voice and is known for disruption, debauchery, and his fondness for tobacco and rum. As head of the Gede family of lwa, he has the final say on death itself and is also a healer and protector of souls. His consort is Maman Brigitte. Baron Samedi can grant resurrection as well as death, making him a figure of both fear and respect.
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Bijan and Manijeh
summary: Bijan, a knight of Iran and son of Giv, is sent to drive wild boars from Armenian fields. Afterward, the envious knight Gorgin tempts Bijan to cross into enemy Turan by describing the beautiful gardens of Afrasiab. Bijan meets Manijeh, the daughter of Afrasiab, and they fall in love. She drugs him and smuggles him into her chambers, but Afrasiab discovers them and chains Bijan in a dungeon. Manijeh is exiled to wander the wilderness. The Iranian king Kay Khosrow sees Bijan in his crystal cup and sends Rostam disguised as a merchant into Turan. With Manijeh's help, Rostam frees Bijan and returns him to Iran, where Manijeh becomes his wife.

title: Bahram Gur (Bahram V, the Hunter King)
summary: The Sassanid king Bahram V (Bahram Gur), renowned for his love of hunting (especially onager, giving him the epithet 'Gur' meaning onager), is the subject of numerous Persian legends. His exploits include hunting with a lion and an onager as companions, his romance with the Indian princess Sapinud (whom he marries after killing a lion and a dragon), and his building of the seven pavilions for his seven princesses (the subject of Nizami's Haft Paykar). Bahram embodies the ideal Sassanid king: martial prowess, justice, and cultural patronage. The story of 'Bahram and the Dragon' recounts his killing a dragon to save a beautiful maiden.
